title: All-Pairs Min-Cut vs. All-Pairs Shortest-Path
speaker: Amir Abboud
abstract:
The All-Pairs Min-Cut problem (APMC) asks to compute the minimum cut (or equivalently, the maximum flow) between all pairs of nodes in a graph.1 The naive solution of making n^2 calls to a single-pair min-cut algorithm was surpassed in 1961 by a remarkable algorithm of Gomory and Hu that requires only n-1 calls. Within the same time bound, their algorithm produces a cut-equivalent tree (or GH-Tree) that preserves all pairwise minimum cuts exactly.2 This yields a cubic upper bound for APMC, which has stood as the barrier for the general problem for over 60 years.
The All-Pairs Shortest-Path problem (APSP) appears similar, asking to compute the distance rather than the connectivity between all pairs. Its complexity landscape also seems analogous: despite the existence of mildly subcubic algorithms, a truly subcubic solution remains elusive. In fact, in the past 10 years, the conjecture that APSP requires essentially cubic time has played a central role in fine-grained complexity, leading to conditional lower bounds for many other fundamental problems that appear even easier than APMC. However, a formal reduction from APSP to APMC has remained elusive.
This talk will overview a sequence of works (with Krauthgamer, Li, Panigrahi, Saranurak, and Trabelsi) breaking the 60-year-old cubic barrier for APMC, establishing a counter-intuitive separation between APMC and APSP, and inspiring new attacks on the APSP conjecture. Along the way, we will highlight the use of techniques such as expander decompositions, regularity lemmas, and theorems from additive combinatorics.

proof_stages.label: Baseline comparison of shortest paths and min-cuts
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up single-pair, single-source, and all-pairs shortest-path versus min-cut/max-flow problems, explaining that Dijkstra gives all-pairs shortest paths in cubic time while Gomory-Hu trees give all-pairs undirected min-cuts in essentially the same cubic bound.

proof_stages.label: Fine-grained expectation that all-pairs max-flow is harder
proof_stages.summary: Evidence from unweighted graphs, directed graphs, and the APSP fine-grained conjecture motivates the initial program of proving all-pairs max-flow at least as hard as all-pairs shortest paths.

proof_stages.label: Reversal via faster all-pairs min-cut algorithms
proof_stages.summary: Recent work first breaks the Gomory-Hu cubic barrier for unweighted graphs and then computes weighted Gomory-Hu trees in near-quadratic, later near-linear and deterministic, time, reversing the expected separation under the APSP conjecture.

proof_stages.label: Three-component algorithmic framework
proof_stages.summary: The speaker organizes the min-cut algorithms, and possible APSP attacks, into combinatorial algorithmics, structural analysis of the input, and a heavy black-box tool such as a single-pair max-flow algorithm.

proof_stages.label: Reduction from Gomory-Hu construction to single-source cuts
proof_stages.summary: Starting from the classical Gomory-Hu recursion and its submodularity-based contractions, the talk explains that balanced progress fails for single-pair cuts but would be restored by random pivots if one could compute all single-source min-cuts efficiently.

proof_stages.label: Structural computation of single-source min-cuts
proof_stages.summary: Expander decompositions, tree-packing ideas in the weighted case, and the isolating-cuts subroutine reduce the single-source cut task to only polylogarithmically many max-flow computations on appropriately simplified instances.

proof_stages.label: APSP conjecture as the next target
proof_stages.summary: The min-cut separation motivates attacking APSP itself, whose hard core can be expressed through Boolean and min-plus matrix multiplication, yielding two routes: new combinatorial Boolean multiplication algorithms or reductions that remove/handle weights.

proof_stages.label: Regularity and additive-combinatorial attacks on APSP
proof_stages.summary: Recent progress is framed as structural analysis: weak regularity decompositions improve combinatorial Boolean matrix multiplication, while additive-combinatorial tools such as BSG and related conjectures clarify which weighted APSP instances remain hard.

examples.name: Expander as worst case for naive Gomory-Hu recursion
examples.note: An expander graph illustrates why single-pair Gomory-Hu recursion may peel off only one vertex at a time, leading directly to the idea of using single-source cuts to learn many cuts at once.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker reasons through an expander in Gomory-Hu recursion, explaining that pair cuts isolate one vertex, have degree value, and therefore shrink the instance only by one

passes.reason: An expander has extreme high-expansion cut structure and is used as the adversarial case where naive Gomory-Hu recursion makes only one-vertex progress.

examples.name: Triples recovered by sampled isolating cuts

examples.name: Behrend-type construction as a regularity barrier
examples.note: The Behrend construction is invoked as a structured object that can look regular under many notions while retaining hidden dependence, suggesting a barrier and motivating different regularity notions or restricted APSP variants.
